coredata.dc:title: Plasma-enabled graphene quantum dot-based nanofiltration membranes for water purification and dye monitoring
coredata.dc:creator: Yeh, Yi-Jui
Lin, Wei
Chiang, Wei-Hung
Tung, Kuo-Lun
coredata.dc:description: Nanofiltration (NF) is one of the key technologies for wastewater recycling and clean water production. However, current nanofiller-based NF membranes have been hampered by the inefficient separation due to the low water transportation and self-fouling of the membranes. Here we report a unique self-diagnosis bioresource-derived nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)-based thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane with controlled optical properties and tuned porous nanostructures for ultrahigh water transportation and purification. Our detailed experimental and calculation studies suggest that the NGQDs with nanoscale sizes and abundant surface functional groups can induce the formation of desired porous structures during the membrane fabrication, leading an ultrahigh water permeability of 289 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 (LMHB) and remarkable 99.96% separation efficiency of various organic dyes. Moreover, the stable photoluminescence (PL) emission of NGQD membranes enables the PL-based monitoring of dye-fouling during the water purification. Our work provides a green manufacturing of nanomaterials-based membranes with defined structures and an understating of molecular transport in nanoscale.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofiltration
Quantum dots
Nanocomposite
Bioresources
Nanotechnology
Plasma engineering

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: NGQD NF membrane fabrication and characterization
Theoretical calculation study of NGQD membranes
PL-based dye monitoring study of NGQD membranes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Conventional IP process versus NGQD-enhanced IP process for NF membrane fabrication. (a) In the conventional IP process, the polymerization occurs quickly at the interface between TMC and PIP to form a dense PA layer, as shown in (b and c). Due to the rapid reaction, it is difficult to control the porous structures of the pristine PA membranes without special treatments. (d) In our developed NGQD-enhanced IP process, the rich surface-functionalized NGQDs can induce a self-assembled polymer network, leading controllable porous structures in the NGQD-PA membranes, as shown in (e and f). The NGQDs can act as the nanospacers to facilitate the generation of loosely structured nanofilms with enhanced intrapore interconnectivity (f), which can improve the water transport and separation efficiency of NF membranes.

Morphology and topology of the pristine and modified membrane. (a) SEM images of prepared NGQD membranes (scale bar: 1 μm). (b) XRD patterns of prepared NGQD membranes. (c) Calculated pore size distributions of NGQD membranes. (d) XPS survey spectra of prepared NGQD membranes.

Nanofiltration performance of NGQD membranes. (a) Water permeability and separation performances of t NGQD membranes. (b) Water permeability and MLB separation performances of 0.8% NGQD membrane and PA membrane. (c) The NF performance of the membranes compared with those of other membranes in literatures (see the more detail in supporting information Table S6). (d) Water permeability and separation performances of 0.8% NGQD membrane for different of dye testing (e) Separation performance of 0.8% NGQD membrane for salts of different molecular sizes. (f) Water permeability and rejection of NGQD membranes under different pH values. (g). Stability test of the 0.8% NGQD membrane in a long-time filtration of 1000 ppm MLB.

Theoretical calculation study of the NGQD membranes. (a) Schematic illustration of the MD compressible and relaxation procedure for GQD composite membrane (water phase (GQD, PIP)) and oil phase (TMC)). (b) MSD simulation of the migration H2O molecule in the PA and x-GQD membrane. (c) Molecular models of TFC membranes based on cross-linked PA, 0.2% GQD-PA m 0.6% GQD-PA and 0.6% GQD-PA, where gray areas represent voids (free volume) in each matrix and the blue areas represent the access to the empty accessible entrance. (d) Cavity size distribution of the PA and x-GQD-PA membrane is referred to GQD-embed enlarge the pore size and increase the accessible free volume. (e) RDF of attraction functional groups on the GQD membrane (determine the potential density of the atoms of the water molecule at a given distance). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

PL spectroscopy study of dye monitoring using NGQD membranes. (a) Schematic of PL-based dye monitoring using NGQD membranes. (b) PL maps for NGQD membranes with varying filtration time. The inset shows photographs of NGQD membranes with increasing filtration time (left to right) under UV light irradiation. (c) Reusability test of NGQD membranes using PL measurement during nanofiltration.

Plasma engineering of bioresource-derived NGQD-based NF membranes towards high water transport and purification. (a) The photoluminescent-active and surface-functionalized NGQDs are synthesized by an atmospheric-pressure microplasma engineering using bioresource chitosan as the single carbon and nitrogen-containing source under ambient conditions. (b) Light-weight, flexible, and porous NGQD-based NF membranes were fabricated via an easy-operated and scalable interfacial polymerization of piperazine (PIP) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) as the polymer precursors. The plasma-engineered NGQD-based NF membranes process the exceptional properties including high water permeability, remarkable separation efficiency of organic dyes, and PL-based self-monitoring of dye-fouling during the water purification.
Surface chemical components of NGQD membranes with different NGQD loading percentages. MWCO is defined as the molecular weight cut off and was determined from the rejection curve in Fig. S15.

head.ce:title: Plasma-enabled graphene quantum dot-based nanofiltration membranes for water purification and dye monitoring

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei, 10607, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei 10607, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607 Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofiltration (NF) is one of the key technologies for wastewater recycling and clean water production. However, current nanofiller-based NF membranes have been hampered by the inefficient separation due to the low water transportation and self-fouling of the membranes. Here we report a unique self-diagnosis bioresource-derived nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)-based thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane with controlled optical properties and tuned porous nanostructures for ultrahigh water transportation and purification. Our detailed experimental and calculation studies suggest that the NGQDs with nanoscale sizes and abundant surface functional groups can induce the formation of desired porous structures during the membrane fabrication, leading an ultrahigh water permeability of 289 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 (LMHB) and remarkable 99.96% separation efficiency of various organic dyes. Moreover, the stable photoluminescence (PL) emission of NGQD membranes enables the PL-based monitoring of dye-fouling during the water purification. Our work provides a green manufacturing of nanomaterials-based membranes with defined structures and an understating of molecular transport in nanoscale.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanofiltration (NF) is an energy-efficient membrane separation technology for water treatment and is useful for various applications including semiconductor and optoelectronics manufacturing, pharmaceutical and biomaterials industries, food safety, and pollution control [1–5]. Despite the enormous effect that has been devoted to producing NF membranes by interfacial polymerization method [6,7], a low-cost, environmentally-friendly and scalable technology to fabricate NF membranes with controllable pore structures and topology for high water transport and exceptional separation performance is still not available [8–10]. Moreover, membrane fouling due to contamination can degrade membrane performance and increase operating costs, making it difficult to develop NF membranes with both high separation efficiency and long-term stability.

To overcome the problem, we propose a nanotechnology-enhanced solution by engineering the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD)-based NF membranes with useful properties of high water transport and photoluminescence (PL)-based dye detection. Low-dimensional nanomaterials including nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes (CNT), nanowires, 2D nanosheets such as graphene oxides and Mxene nanosheets are effective materials to enhance the performance of NF membranes [11–14]. The introduction of those nanomaterials can generate abundant low-resistance nanochannels for water transport into the active layer through its inherent nanopores and interfacial voids, which greatly improves water flow [15–17]. Recently, GQDs have been reported as emerging carbon nanomaterials with exceptional quantum-induced properties including high surface area, abounded surface functionalities, high PL stability and tunable emission, biocompatibility, making it potential materials for nanocatalysis, electronics, clean energy and nanomedicines [18,19]. Additionally, the incorporation of hydrophilic carbon-based nanodots into NF membranes can improve surface hydrophilicity, resulting in high water fluxes [20]. Furthermore, the GQDs with superior PL properties can be used to fabricate porous optical-active nanocomposites for ultrasensitive and rapid detection of water contaminants such as dyes, metal ions, and organic compounds [21–23]. Together with the abovementioned useful properties of GQDs, it is desired to develop a GQD-based NF membranes and study their water transport property and PL-based monitoring of the contamination during the separation.

GQDs can be prepared by both top-down and bottom-up methods. However, these methods usually involve toxic reagents and chemicals, time-consuming and laborious procedures, and expensive manufacturing equipment [24–26]. Plasma-based technology, especially atmospheric-pressure microplasmas, has been reported an effective method to produce nanomaterials including metal nanoparticles, semiconductor quantum dots and graphene-based nanomaterials due to the high chemical reactivities provide by the reactive ions and radicals generate in the plasmas [27]. Moreover, it is possible to synthesis GQDs and doped heteroatoms into GQDs in one step using microplasmas, making it useful for mass production of GQDs in a scalable and green manner.

In this work, we report a green and scalable bioresource-derived synthesis of nitrogen-doped GQD (NGQD)-based NF membranes with high water transport and PL-based properties (Scheme 1 ). The NGQDs were prepared by atmospheric pressure microplasma engineering using chitosan as the single carbon and nitrogen-containing source under ambient conditions (Scheme 1a). During the synthesis, no toxic chemicals, strong acid, vacuum conditions and high temperature were used. Light-weight, flexible, and porous NGQD-based NF membranes were fabricated via an easy-operated and scalable interfacial polymerization of piperazine (PIP) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) as the polymer precursors (Scheme 1b). We discover that the plasma NGQDs with rich surface functionalities and nitrogen dopants can tune the surface morphology, hydrophilicity and pore structures of the NF membranes based on our detailed experimental and calculation studies. Moreover, systematic water transport and purification measurements indicate that the NGQD-based NF membranes can achieve an extremely high water flux of 289 L m−2 h−1 bar (LMHB) and exceptional rejection of 99.96% for various organic dyes. Furthermore, the stable PL emission of NGQD membranes enables the monitoring of dye-fouling during the water purification, showing a potential for industrial setting. Our work provides a step to understand the insight of water transport in GQD-based NF membranes and green manufacturing for GQD-based nanocomposites for environmental safety, biomedical applications, catalysis, and clean energy.

The NGQDs were initially synthesized from chitosan as the precursor under ambient conditions using a customized microplasma engineering [27–29]. Details are provided in the Supporting Information (Section S1-2 ). Supported membranes (wet thickness of 250 μm) were first fabricated and stored in deionized water. The net water flux from the supported membrane is approximately 180 LMHB at 0.1 MPa, and the rejection of BSA (67 kDa) by the supported membrane is approximately 98%. A series of GQD-PA membranes were then supported by interfacial polymerization (IP) of PIP and TMC in the membranes with GQDs as aqueous additives. The supported membranes were soaked with an aqueous solution containing 0.1 wt% PIP and a fixed amount of GQD for 5 min. Then the membranes were carefully removed and immersed in a 0.1 wt% oil phase solution for 2 min to form a PA layer by IP process [30–32]. Finally, the resulting TFN films were dried in a 60 °C oven for 5 min. The NGQD-PA membranes with different NGQD amounts are listed in the Supporting Information Table S1.

The prepared samples were characterized using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopies. Absorption spectra were recorded using a JASCO V676 absorption spectrophotometer. TEM was performed using a cold-field emission Cs-corrected TEM (JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. AFM was carried out by Ardic Instruments P-100 at a scanning frequency of 0.8–1.0 Hz in constant contact scanning mode. FTIR was carried by a Nicolet FTIR-iS10 in ATR mode. XPS was additionally performed using a VG ESCA Scientific Theta Probe. PL spectroscopy measurements were performed at room temperature with a commercial spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Nanolog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer). Details can be found in the Supporting Information (Section S3).

Membrane separation performance, including water flow and contaminant/salt separation, was evaluated using a final mix filter equipped with a nitrogen bottle and solution reservoir. The experiments were performed at a low pressure of 0.5 MPa and an effective membrane area of 28.7 cm 2 based on the previous work for nanofiltration study [15,33]. The water flow was measured after pre-compacting the membrane at 2.5 MPa for 0.5 h and a stable value was obtained. Pure water consumption Jw is calculated with the following formula of
J w
=
V
A Δ T
(eq.1), where V (L) is the volume of water permeated, A (m2) is the membrane area and ΔT (h) is the permeation time. Using Evans Blue (EB, 960Da), Kangaroo Red (CR, 697 Da), Victoria Blue B (VB, 506 Da), Crystal Violet (CV, 408 Da), Methyl Orange (MO, 327 Da), Methylene Blue (MLB, 319 Da) and Na2SO4 in saline solution (0.1 g/L, pH = 7.0). The rejection ratios (R) were calculated by equation of
R =
C f
−
C p
C f
× 100 %
(eq 0.2), where Cp (g/l) and Cf (g/l) were the concentrations of the permeate and stock solutions. Salt concentration was measured by electrical conductivity (MCP-610). Dye concentrations were measured using a spectrophotometer in the ultraviolet and visible regions (JASCO V676 absorbance spectroscopy).

The molecular weight cutoff value (MWCO), the mean effective pore size (mcp), and the pore size distribution of membranes were characterized in solute suppression experiments (200, 400, 600, 1000, 2000 and 4000 Da). The coefficient of variation (Rp) of PEG (100 ppm, pH = 7.0) was measured with a chemical oxygen demand analyzer (COD-571, Shanghai Leichi Instrument Co.) and calculated by the formula of
R p
=
C f ′
−
C p ′
C f ′
× 100 %
(e q.3), where C'f and C'p are the solute concentrations in the feed and permeate, respectively [6,34]. Membrane MWCO is defined as the molecular weight of the solute at which membrane rejection reaches 90%. The solution diameter (dp, nm) was calculated from the PEG molecular weight (Mw, g/mol) according to the equation of
d R

(e q.4), where the mean effective pore size (μp) was found to be dp at Rp = 50.0%, and the geometric standard deviation (σp) was found to be the ratio of dp at Rp = 84.1% and 50.0%. The pore size distribution of the substrate can then be obtained by the following probability density function of
d p
= 33.5 ×
10
− 3
M w
0.557
.

Six models of membranes with or without crust, GQD and PA composites (PIP and TMC) were built for ab initio calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. All molecular models in this work are built by the commercial software, Materials Studio 6.0 (Accelrys Inc., San Diego). MD simulations were used to study the effect of rigid leather sections on membrane structure, water molecular diffusion, and membrane behavior during H2O transport. The detailed information is provided in the Supporting Information (Section S4-S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NGQD NF membrane fabrication and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The motivation of this work is to synthesis NGQD NF membranes with different NGQD mass loading up to 1.0% and systematically study their water transport properties. The NGQDs were initially synthesized from bioresource chitosan as the precursor under ambient conditions using microplasmas [27,35,36]. Details of the GQD synthesis and characterization are provided in the Supporting Information (Section S1-3 and Figs. S1–S5). The NGQD-based membranes were prepared using a low-cost, simple-to-operate scalable interfacial polymerization (IP) process [30]. Fig. 1 a shows that the IP reaction can occur within seconds, forming a dense selective layer with high repulsion for ions. Due to the monomer effect and fast reaction, it is difficult for traditional IP reactions to achieve ideal permeability and selectivity. The PA (pristine polyamide) active layer (Fig. 1b and c) was formed on the porous dendrimer layer using PIP and TMC to form the dense amide layer (Fig. S6). To engineer the structures of the quantum-based graphene active layer, we preload the NGQD during the IP process to induce loose wrinkled and specific flower-like structures on the porous support membrane, enhancing the hydrophilicity and larger surface area of prepared NGQD membranes (Fig. 1d and e). Moreover, the NGQD nanofillers embedded in the PA matrix can act as nanospacers to crate the loose structure improved connectivity in the pores [37], thus contribute to perfect compatibility of the PIP interface (Fig. 1f). Prepared GQD-PA membranes may offer a versatile and promising toward facilitate molecular separation and high-performance nanofiltration applications, as well as in situ quantum dot-enabled pollution monitoring in filtration applications.

SEM was used to understand the effect of the PA active layer and the loading mass of NGQDs on the structure of the PA active layer. The layered and irregular macroporous structures in the 200–500 nm range of the PES substrate is observed (Fig. S7). After the IP reaction, due to the positive capillary force, all macropores were evenly distributed when immersed in the PIP/TMC solution, followed by a relatively non-rough and flawless polymer layer (Fig. S8). In contrast, the NGQD-PA membranes exhibited a more pronounced wrinkled surface morphology with flower-like nanostructured protrusions (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S9). It is noted that the membrane surfaces become rougher, and the filtering areas expand as the amount of NGQDs increases. As the NGQD concentration reaches 0.6%, the wrinkle-like structures are generated. Moreover, under the 0.8% NGQD concentration, floral and wrinkle structures are constructed. Furthermore, when 1% NGQD is added, partial aggregation and pore expansion are observed. The synthesized NGQDs are rich with polar surface functionalities and nitrogen dopants, making it possible to achieve a good dispersion in PIP solution. We have additionally performed the microRaman spectroscopy to study that the NGQD distribution in the PIP solution. The presence of homogeneous NGQD signals in the microRaman spectra indicates that NGQDs are dispersed uniformly in the PIP solution (Fig. S10). Moreover, higher filler mass of GQDs leads to tighter structures. AFM images show the average roughness profile (Ra) differences for PA active layers with GQD nominal loading mass 0.2%, 0.4%, 0.6%, 0.8% and 1.0% were 6.55, 8.65, 10.32, 15.12 and 17.78, respectively, suggesting that the surface roughness of membrane was increased with increased GQD nominal loading mass (Fig. S11 and Table S2). Since interfacial polymerization reaction plays an essential role on diffusion-controlled process, it is hypothesized that NGQDs could act as nanofiller with suitable chemical and dimensional properties to induce roughness and specific wrinkle and flower-like structures (Fig. S9 (d) and (e) and Fig. S12) [38], which could improve the solvent transport during separation process [39,40]. In general, the interaction of NGQDs with the PA can be improved by the NGQD surface functional groups including carboxylic acid, epoxy, and hydroxyl groups, leading a thin layer of PA to be generated around the NGQDs. As more NGQDs are introduced during the IP process, some NGQDs are aggregated and therefore reduce the diffusion rate of PA monomer in the IP process. Hence flower-like and wrinkle fold structures can be constructed in the IP process.

To understand the thicknesses and porosities of NGQD membranes, cross-sectional SEM images were collected. From the SEM images (Fig. S13), it is indicated that NGQD membrane exhibit thin microporous structures with intrinsic intra-pore interconnecting. The NGQD functional groups could react strongly with TFC due to the confinement effect, steric hindrance and the functional group interaction between the built-in nanofillers and the PIP resulting in an extended structure [41–43]. The formation of internal voids in PA films is mainly attributed to the intercalation of hydrophilic GQD nanofillers, where the absorbed water tends to hydrolyze acid chlorides to carboxyl groups and avoid the formation of polyamide in its environment [44,45]. XRD and FTIR were further performed to study crystalline structures and chemical bonds of the prepared NGQD membranes. The XRD pattern of NGQD membranes all display diffraction peak smaller than 23.14o, which was larger than the (002) spacing of graphite (0.34 nm) (Fig. 2b) [46,47]. The increase of interlayer distance was mainly owing to incompletely carbonized PIP chains. The FTIR CO and N–H peaks at 1650 and 1608 cm−1, respectively are the characteristic peaks of polyamide polymer (Fig. S14) [48,49]. It is showed that the N–H peak decreased as more NGQDs were conjugated with PA, confirming the decreased cross-linking degree. As the NGQDs contents gradually increased from 0 to 1.0%, the pore size probability density curve shifts towards large average pore sizes and relatively lower probability densities (Fig. 2c). This suggests that the intercalation of NGQDs can facilitate the generation of larger pores and wide pore size distributions.

To study the chemical composition of the prepared NGQD membranes, XPS was utilized. The NGQD membranes contains C, O and N elements, attributing from the formed PA selective layer on the PES support (Fig. 2d). The degree of cross-linking of the PA polymer layer can be calculated from the atomic percentages of N and O (Fig. S15 and Table 1 and Table S3) according to previous work [45,50,51]. The results indicate that the NH2 and COOH functional groups rise marginally with the addition of GQD filler. The primary explanation is that GQD is rich in functional groups, therefore when it induces PA formation, it also alters the surface functional groups. Generally, higher O/N and O/C ratio present a lower degree of crosslinking of the PA layer. The degree of cross-linking of the selected layers is related to the NGQDs content. Compared to the other films, the 1.0% NGQD film shows significantly higher O/N and O/C ratios. It is assumed that an excess of NGQDs can significantly reduce the degree of crosslinking of the PA layer, which can be confirmed by the reduction in dye filtration. Based on the group functionalization by microplasma treatment, the GQD-PA membrane contains more COOH and NH2 bonds, which can reduce the contact angle with water embedded in the PA membrane, thus achieving the performance of ultrahigh nanofiltration and high pore size precision.

First, we performed a 24-h NGQD leaching test utilizing PL spectroscopy. NGQD was not leached from the polymer membranes, as indicated by the absence of NGQD emission peak in the PL spectrum of the test solution (Fig. S16). To study the nanofiltration of the prepared NGQD membranes, a series of filtration measurements were performed. It is found that 0.8% NGQD membranes can reach 99.96% rejection rate of MLB and 289 LMHB water flux due to the NGQD-induced porous structures of NGQD membranes (Fig. 3 a). While the highest water flux can be observed for the 1 wt% NGQD membrane, the rejection rate was largely reduced. Pristine PA membrane was also prepared at the same conditions, and its water permeability was only 7 LMHB, as shown in Fig. 3b. Based on the above results, we chose 0.8% NGQD membranes as a model to further understand the fundamental of water transport and nanofiltration. In addition, we addressed the possibility of adsorption in high-performance nanofiltration. The UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy suggests there is no detected adsorption of dyes on GQDs (Fig. S17). Moreover, the zeta potential measurement indicates that both the dye and GQD membrane are the same negative charge, suggesting that the adsorption of dyes on GQD membranes could not be happened because of the repulsive electrostatic force (Fig. S18). Molecular selectivity is essential for nanofiltration. Here we have selected a series of dyes and salts with different molecules weights (319.85–960 Da), including evans blue (EB, 960 Da), methyl blue (MB, 799 Da), fuchsic acid (VFA, 585 Da), orange GII (OG, 452 Da), methyl orange (MO, 327 Da), methylene blue (MLB, 319 Da), anhydrous sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) and sodium chloride (NaCl). Tables S4 and S5 showed the detailed chemical structures, surface energies and molecular sizes. As shown in Fig. 3d and e, it is indicated that NGQD-membranes shows remarked 289 LMHB water flux and >98% rejection for MLB, MO, CV, VB, CR, EB, and Na2SO4. The high zeta value strengthens the rejection for divalent molecules as a result of the high rejection for Na2SO4 (Fig. S18). In addition, the addition of NGQDs can increase the hydrophilic characteristics of the membrane surfaces, hence controlling the porosity of the membranes and greatly enhancing the filtering performance of dibasic salt. However, for NaCl with a small molecular size of 0.07 nm, the GQD membrane showed only 23% rejection due to inefficient sieving effect on very small molecules. In addition, the separation of mixed solutions of MLB/NaCl was carried out. The result showed that the NGQD membrane can maintained 98% inhibition of MLB and 27% inhibition of NaCl, which matched the one-component solution filtration assay. The nanofiltration membrane at extreme pH values is essential for practical applications. Herein, we performed the performance of NGQD membranes at pH values in harsh environment, as shown in Fig. 3f. The NGQD membranes significantly show ∼289 LMHB and ∼99% rejection under from pH 2 to 10, displaying good pH stability. Long-term stability of NGQD membranes was also achieved within 60 h as shown in Fig. 3g. The initial water permanent of the PA membrane was only 7 LMHB, and then rapidly decreased to 3 LMHB due to the formation of a dye cake. In contrast, NGQDs can induce the permanent high-velocity channels along curved surfaces, reaching the value of 206 LMHB in 60 h after steady state operated work [52,53]. Fig. 3c and Table S6 summarize the filtration performance of our NGQD membranes and other nanomaterials-based membranes in literatures. Overall, the NGQD membranes processes outstanding nanofiltration performance including high water flux and dye rejection rate, good long-term and pH stabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculation study of NGQD membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the effect of NGQDs on the dynamic behavior of water motion in our work (Fig. 4 a), we have established a series of simulations to elucidate the properties of prepared NGQD membranes. We have established a series of simulations to elucidate the properties of prepared NGQD membranes (Table S7). Detail is provided in Table S8 and Fig. S19. Fig. 4b presents the plot of mean square displacement (MSD) versus time for water molecules at 298.15 K. The correlation coefficients in each figure are all greater than 0.99 and the curve fit is reasonable. The data shows that models integrating NGQDs into PA have lower resistance to the passage of water molecules and lower diffusion coefficients than models without NGQDs [54,55]. This indicates that the diffusivity of water molecules leads to higher water flux, which proves that the addition of NGQDs can effectively promote the diffusion of water molecules in the dye solutions.

To reveal the micropore effect in NGQD membranes, the fractional free volume (FFV) in the NPT group (NTP status; constant pressure-temperature status) was calculated during a 1000 ps MD simulation. The FFV values for each membrane were stable, indicating that an equilibrium structure for each membrane has been approach. Trigger bridges unit in NGQD-modified PA films have higher FFVs than pristine PA membranes, representing that trigger bridges can destroy polymer chain stacking [56]. In addition, rigid NGQD materials were found to generate a larger free volume for better nanofiltration performance (Fig. S13 and Table S9). It is noted that NGQDs can enhance the FFV of the membranes, especially leading a significant improvement from 0.6 to 0.8%. Accordingly, the experiment demonstrates that the water flux is efficiently and greatly increased from 80 LMHB to 298 LMHB.

To further investigate the mechanism leading to the superiority of NGQD-PA over PA films, a NGQD-based cross-linked network was constructed to model the reaction, and internal voids (free volume) were analyzed as shown in Fig. 4c. The NGQD-PA network has a larger internal cavity (889.3923 nm3 (1%), shown in gray), with easier access to water molecules (shown in blue) [57]. Compared to the PA grating (513.4193 nm3), resulting in an almost double increase in the size of internal voids (Fig. S20). Moreover, the increase in NGQD loading in the PA film indicates that the internal void enhancement is compatible with NGQDs due to the monomeric IP response. The results of the MD simulation are in good agreement with those obtained from the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) results (Fig. S21).

We also consider the pore size distribution in the NGQD membranes, which is associated with the water molecular transport properties. The pore size measurements were studied using an image analysis algorithm. The imaging results of the six membranes are shown in Figs. S22–S25. As seen in Fig. 4d, the traveling unit can improve the large pore size distribution. It was found that the resistance of the cavity size increases in the following order: Pristine PA < 0.2% NGQD membrane <0.4% NGQD membrane <0.6% NGQD membrane <0.8% NGQD membrane <1.0% NGQD membrane on large area. This trend is similar to the experimental data. Although larger pores result in higher transmittance, this depends on the size of the dye. In the testing results, the optimal value of the NGQD membrane is 0.8%, which achieves highest dye rejection.

To understand the effect of functional groups on H2O trapping, 100H2O molecules were placed in the simulation box and MD simulations were run for 100 ps. The radial distribution function (RDF) of functional groups in 0.8% NGQD membranes was analyzed (Fig. 4e). RDF for each functional groups to attract the H2O molecule. These peaks are all concentrated around the radius between the H2O molecule and the 2 or 4 Å functional group, indicating hydrophilicity. Pyrrolic N also showed significant attraction to H2O. From this figure, it can be seen that the affinity of the functional group of NGQD membranes for the H2O molecule is better than that of the functional group of PA, thus affecting the interaction with H2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL-based dye monitoring study of NGQD membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To study the PL-based dye monitoring using the prepared NGQD membranes, detailed PL spectroscopy study was conducted. The purpose is to monitor the process of dye cake formation on the NGQD membrane surfaces. PL spectroscopy is a useful tool to monitor the fluorescence signals of quantum material embedded in the polymer composites (Fig. 5 a) [58]. First it is observed that the PL intensity of the NGQD membranes at the characteristic emission wavelength of NGQD was gradually decreased with increasing filtration time. Moreover, the quenching of PL film can be easily observed with the naked eye under the UV light irradiation, as shown in the photographs in Fig. 5b. The above results suggest that encapsulated NGQDs can act as sensitive fluorescent probes for rapid dye-cake detection. To quantify the PL intensity associated with water performance as shown in Fig. S26. Fig. 5c shows the positive correlation between filtration time and PL intensity over 10 h. Between the two cycles, the membrane was rinsed with ethanol for several minutes to remove the filter cake and restore water flow to its original level. The second, third and fourth cycle ability has been maintained at nearly 99%, showing that the NGQD membrane with excellent recyclability and dye-cake rejection. This property is beneficial to the nanofiltration of dyes and practical long-term usage in monitoring of the repair process. We have additionally measured the PL properties of the studied dyes including cango red, methylene blue, crystal violet, evans blue, rhodamine B and rhodamine 6G (Fig. S27). It is indicated that the PL emissions of the abovementioned dyes are far away with the emission of the fabricated NGQD membranes, showing that it is possible to use our fabricated NGQD membrane to monitor the dye separation. It is suggested that the dyes with PL emissions not overlapped with our NGQD membranes of 400 nm emission can be monitored in our work.

In conclusion, we have reported the plasma-engineered bioresource-derived NGQD-based TFN membranes with stable optical properties and tuned porous nanostructures for water purification and dye monitoring. The microplasma method is effective to engineer the surface functional groups on the NGQDs derived from chitosan, assisting the membrane fabrication through the interfacial polymer process. The porous nanostructures can be turned by controlling the NGQD density in the membranes, leading a remarkable water permeability of 289 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and selectivity up to 99.96% in the size-exclusion separation of methylene blue. The modeling studies suggest that the GQD-induced porous nanostructures can be the key factor to control the water transportation within the membranes during clean water production. The stable PL property of NGQD membranes allows PL-based monitoring of dye-fouling during the clean water production. Our work provides a step offering the NF technology for water treatment, solvent dehydration, and organics sieving.
Additional data including PL, UV, SEM and simulation in detail. This material is available free of charge via the Internet.

Yi-Jui Yeh: Writing - Original Draft, Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization. Wei Lin: Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis. Wei-Hung Chiang: Writing - Review & Editing, Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Data Curation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Kuo-Lun Tung Writing - Review & Editing, Resources, Supervision, and Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thankfully acknowledge the financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology in Taiwan (MOST 111-2223-E-011-002-MY3, 111-2628-E-011-002-MY2, 109-2923-E-011-003-MY3, 111-NU-E-011-001-NU). We also thank Ms. Chia-Ying Chien of MOST (National Taiwan University) for the assistance in FE-TEM experiment.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.memsci.2022.121334.
